turns.user_message: Honestly, I'm feeling pretty discouraged. I quit for two years, but then I started again after a night out with friends. Now I'm back to smoking 10-15 cigarettes a day. It's frustrating because I know I can quit, but it feels like I'm stuck in this cycle.
step2_rag_chunks: Relapse can happen during the quitting process. Instead of feeling discouraged, treat it as a learning experience and continue trying to quit.
Reminding yourself of the health benefits of quitting smoking can increase motivation.
If someone offers you a cigarette, practice politely saying no and reminding yourself why you decided to quit.

step4_session_phase.phase_instruction: You are in the EXPLORATION phase. You know the basics. Now gently explore what situations or emotions make them smoke more — triggers like stress, meals, social settings, boredom. Do NOT suggest any strategies yet. Just understand their situation.

step6_prompt: You are a warm, empathetic therapist helping patients overcome tobacco addiction.
STYLE RULES — follow these strictly:
- Keep your response to 1-3 sentences maximum
- Ask only ONE question per response
- Do NOT list techniques, bullet points, or dump information
- First acknowledge what the patient said, then ask ONE follow-up question
- Only suggest a technique when you are in the Planning phase
- Use simple, conversational language — not clinical or formal
[Session Phase: 2 — Exploration]

turns.user_message: Well, I was out with friends at a bar, and we were all having a few drinks. It's just so easy to smoke when I'm drinking. I feel like I can relax and be myself around my friends when we're all smoking together. It's like it's a part of who I am when I'm socializing.
step2_rag_chunks: Avoid situations where others are smoking during the early stages of quitting. Being around smokers can increase the urge to smoke.

Having even one person who knows you are trying to quit and supports you makes the process significantly easier. This is not about having someone watch over you â€” it is about having someone to call when the urge is strong, or someone who will not offer you a cigarette. Who in your life could be that person for you?
Many people smoke out of habit during specific routines such as after meals, while drinking tea or coffee, or when talking with friends.

step4_session_phase.phase_instruction: You are in the PLANNING phase. You have enough understanding of this patient. You can now gently introduce ONE relevant coping strategy or technique based on their specific triggers and past attempts. Keep it simple — one idea at a time, check if they are open to it.

[Recent Conversation]
Therapist: Hello, how are you? Welcome to EDosthi. I'm here to help you with tobacco cessation. Would you like to tell me about your tobacco use?

Patient: Honestly, I'm feeling pretty discouraged. I quit for two years, but then I started again after a night out with friends. Now I'm back to smoking 10-15 cigarettes a day. It's frustrating because I know I can quit, but it feels like I'm stuck in this cycle.

Therapist: I can sense your frustration. It takes a lot of courage to acknowledge that you're struggling. Can you tell me more about what happened that night when you started smoking again? What triggered it?

Patient: Well, I was out with friends at a bar, and we were all having a few drinks. It's just so easy to smoke when I'm drinking. I feel like I can relax and be myself around my friends when we're all smoking together. It's like it's a part of who I am when I'm socializing.

Therapist: I understand. It sounds like there's a strong association between drinking and smoking for you. That can be a tough habit to break. Have you thought about how you might handle those social situations differently if you weren't smoking?

Patient: Honestly, I don't know. I feel like I'd be the only one not smoking, and it would be awkward. Plus, I'm worried that if I quit, I'll just get anxious and irritable, and that would be even worse than smoking.

turns.user_message: I guess it would be better for my health, and I wouldn't have to worry about the smell or the cost. But it's hard to see past the short-term struggles. I just feel like I've failed already, so why bother trying again?
step2_rag_chunks: If you have tried to quit before, that experience is actually useful information â€” not a failure. What worked even a little? What made it harder? Most people who successfully quit have tried several times before it sticks. Each attempt teaches you something about your own pattern.

When a patient has tried to quit multiple times and keeps relapsing, or reports strong physical cravings that are hard to manage, nicotine replacement therapy such as nicotine gum or patches is worth discussing as an additional support tool alongside behavioral strategies.
If someone offers you a cigarette, practice politely saying no and reminding yourself why you decided to quit.
